_comment: Bundled Messier deep-sky catalog (J2000). Static data file, no network. Coordinates are accurate to a fraction of a degree — enough for observability ranking (altitude/transit/window), which is what the night planner uses them for. Sourced from the standard Messier catalog. Fields: id, name (common name or ''), ra_deg, dec_deg, type, con (constellation abbrev), size_arcmin (optional major-axis angular size in arcminutes, for the 'will it fit in one frame?' framing hint; omitted when not vetted).; blurb (optional beginner-friendly one-liner about the object, shown on the 'what am I looking at?' card; omitted when not curated)

objects.name: Crab Nebula
objects.blurb: The wreckage of a star seen to explode in 1054 AD, bright enough that astronomers around the world recorded it. It still glows about 6,500 light-years away in Taurus.

objects.name: Cat's Eye Cluster
objects.blurb: One of the closest globular clusters to us, a loose ball of ancient stars near the bright orange star Antares in Scorpius. Easy to resolve into a sparkle of individual suns.

objects.name: Lagoon Nebula
objects.blurb: A huge, bright star-forming cloud in Sagittarius — one of the showpiece nebulae of the summer Milky Way.

objects.name: Wild Duck Cluster
objects.blurb: A rich, compact swarm of hundreds of stars in Scutum, set against the glow of the Milky Way. Named for a flight of ducks its brightest stars seem to trace.

objects.name: Hercules Cluster
objects.blurb: The finest globular cluster in the northern sky: a tight ball of several hundred thousand ancient stars, about 22,000 light-years away.

objects.name: Eagle Nebula
objects.blurb: A star-forming nebula famous for the towering 'Pillars of Creation' at its heart, made iconic by the Hubble Space Telescope.

objects.name: Omega Nebula
objects.blurb: A bright emission nebula in Sagittarius, also known as the Swan or Checkmark Nebula for its distinctive shape.

objects.name: Trifid Nebula
objects.blurb: A striking star-forming region that pairs pink emission gas with blue reflection nebulosity, split into lobes by dark dust lanes.

objects.name: Sagittarius Cluster
objects.blurb: One of the brightest globular clusters in the sky, a dense ball of hundreds of thousands of stars in Sagittarius. Big and bright — a rewarding, easy target when it clears the horizon.

objects.name: Dumbbell Nebula
objects.blurb: A bright planetary nebula — the glowing shell of gas cast off by a dying Sun-like star, and one of the easiest planetaries to image.

objects.name: Andromeda Galaxy
objects.blurb: The nearest large spiral galaxy to our own, about 2.5 million light-years away — the most distant object most people can see with the naked eye.
A small elliptical galaxy that orbits the giant Andromeda Galaxy, often caught in the same frame.

objects.name: Triangulum Galaxy
objects.blurb: A large face-on spiral galaxy in our Local Group, roughly 2.7 million light-years away — rewarding but faint.

objects.name: Orion Nebula
objects.blurb: The brightest nebula in the sky and a vast stellar nursery about 1,340 light-years away — the classic first target for almost every beginner.

objects.name: De Mairan's Nebula
objects.blurb: A bright knot of the Orion Nebula, separated from the main cloud by a dark lane of dust.

objects.name: Beehive Cluster
objects.blurb: A bright, sprawling open cluster in Cancer, visible to the naked eye as a hazy patch since ancient times.

objects.name: Pleiades
objects.blurb: The Seven Sisters: a young, brilliant open cluster wrapped in wisps of blue reflection nebulosity, about 440 light-years away.

objects.name: Whirlpool Galaxy
objects.blurb: A textbook face-on spiral caught interacting with a smaller companion galaxy — its grand spiral arms are a favourite target.

objects.name: Scorpion Cluster
objects.blurb: A pretty, star-rich open cluster in Cassiopeia, easy to find and up most of the year from northern skies. Sits near the small, glowing Bubble Nebula.

objects.name: Ring Nebula
objects.blurb: A small, bright planetary nebula in Lyra shaped like a smoke ring — the glowing shell of a dying star.

objects.name: Sunflower Galaxy
objects.blurb: A bright spiral galaxy whose many tightly-wound, patchy arms give it a flower-like look.

objects.name: Black Eye Galaxy
objects.blurb: A spiral galaxy with a dramatic dark band of dust across its bright core, giving it a 'black eye'.

objects.name: Phantom Galaxy
objects.blurb: A beautiful but faint face-on 'grand-design' spiral — a rewarding challenge under dark skies.

objects.name: Little Dumbbell Nebula
objects.blurb: A small planetary nebula, a fainter cousin of the Dumbbell, shaped like a cork or peanut.
One of the brightest reflection nebulae in the sky — dust glowing blue by the light of young stars in Orion.

objects.name: Bode's Galaxy
objects.blurb: A bright, well-shaped spiral galaxy in Ursa Major, often imaged alongside its neighbour the Cigar Galaxy (M82).

objects.name: Cigar Galaxy
objects.blurb: An edge-on 'starburst' galaxy erupting with new stars, its centre torn by reddish filaments of glowing hydrogen.

objects.name: Southern Pinwheel Galaxy
objects.blurb: A bright, face-on barred spiral galaxy — a southern-sky showpiece full of pink star-forming regions.

objects.name: Virgo A
objects.blurb: A giant elliptical galaxy at the heart of the Virgo cluster, home to the first black hole ever directly imaged.
A rich globular cluster in Hercules — often overlooked next to its famous neighbour M13, but splendid in its own right.

objects.name: Owl Nebula
objects.blurb: A round planetary nebula whose two dark 'eyes' give it an owl-like face.

objects.name: Pinwheel Galaxy
objects.blurb: A large, face-on spiral galaxy with sweeping arms — bright core, but the faint arms reward long integration.

objects.name: Sombrero Galaxy
objects.blurb: An edge-on spiral with a bright bulge and a sharp dark dust lane, giving it the look of a wide-brimmed hat.
A bright spiral galaxy in Canes Venatici with an active core powered by a supermassive black hole.

objects.name: Surfboard Galaxy
objects.blurb: A long, mottled edge-on galaxy in Ursa Major, near the Owl Nebula.
The second dwarf companion of the Andromeda Galaxy, a soft elliptical glow in the same wide field.
